text:
Admission Date:  2013-11-20              Discharge Date:   1982-05-06


Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillins / Sulfa (Sulfonamides)

Attending:Kelli
Chief Complaint:
SOB (transfer from MICU)

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None


History of Present Illness:
[**Age over 90 **]M h/o CAD s/p MI, CHF, PAF on coumadin who p/w 1 week of
increasing SOB and BRBPR. He first noticed SOB after being
diagnosed with Erlichiosis in 11-02; these symptoms have
significantly worsened over the last 2 weeks. At baseline, he is
quite functional (walks >1 mile, flight of stairs, 20 [**Date Range **] on
treadmill); however, recently notes DOE after walking 1 block.
No orthopnea, PND, chest pain. Did complain of a nonproductive
cough but no fevers, chills, or sick contacts. Denise Russell history of
asthma or COPD and is a lifelong nonsmoker. Has had palpitations
~1x/wk typically in the morning and resolving spontaneously and
was recently diagnosed with new-onset Afib 11-10 after presenting
to ED with RVR and chest pain; he was rate-controlled and
started on coumadin. Finally, the evening prior to admission, he
went to the bathroom and had 1 episode of 'beet-colored' stool.
No further bloody bowel movements since. He presented to the ED
due to shortness of breath.
.
In the ED: T 97.0 BP 127/67 HR 72 RR 30 O2 98% RA. EKG NSR w/
nonspecific ST changes anteriorly. Troponin 0.03 likely due to
demand but CKs negative. CXR showed small R sided pleural
effusion but no infiltrate. Labs significant for INR 2.7, Hct 28
(from 32 10 days prior) and guaiac positive on rectal exam. He
received vit K 5 mg sc. Given concern for GI bleeding, the
patient was admitted to the MICU for close monitoring.
.
At presentation to the MICU, the patient was hypertensive
(150s/110s) and in moderate respiratory distress likely from
acute pulmonary edema. He improved with 1 mg morphine, nitro
gtt, and combivent nebs. He was diuresed with lasix and weaned
off supplemental O2 to room air. The patient developed atrial
flutter with RVR that was difficult to rate control with coreg
and dilt. EP was consulted and he was chemically converted to
NSR with ibutilide and was loaded with amiodarone. While in RVR,
he did complain of chest pain but had no cardiac enzyme leak. GI
was consulted due to G+ stool but given that his Hct remained
stable and there was no hemodynamic compromise, colonoscopy was
deferred. Heparin gtt was restarted and he was transferred to
the floor for further management.

Past Medical History:
# CHF (EF 45-50%)
# CAD s/p MI (1998)
# PAF on coumadin
# Hyperlipidemia
# HTN
# GERD
# Esophageal stricture s/p multiple bouginages Q1-2yrs
# Thyroid nodule s/p thyroidectomy (benign adenoma)
# h/o MGUS
- SPEP w/ 2 abn. bands 08-11- monoclonal IgM kappa
# h/o Erlichiosis infection 11-02 s/p doxycycline x 1 month

Social History:
Social history is significant for the absence of current tobacco
use. There is no history of alcohol abuse; he occasionally
drinks wine.  He is a retired [**University/College 58526**] English
professor. Currently lives in PSC 5331, Box 1627
APO AE 44651 with wife but moving to
Denise Russell Corner soon. Son is an electrician on the Alvarez.

Family History:
There is no family history of premature coronary artery disease
or sudden death. There is a family history of hypothryoidism.


Physical Exam:
T 97.3 RR 20 SpO2 96% on RA. Blood pressure was 122/60 mm Hg
while seated.  Pulse was 74 beats/Parker and regular, respiratory
rate was *** breaths/Parker. Generally the patient was well
developed, well nourished and well groomed. The patient was
oriented to person, place and time. The patient's mood and
affect were not inappropriate.
.
There was no xanthalesma and conjunctiva were pink with no
pallor or cyanosis of the oral mucosa.  The neck was supple with
JVP of 8 cm.  The carotid waveform was normal.  There was no
thyromegaly. The were no chest wall deformities, scoliosis or
kyphosis.  The respirations were not labored and there were no
use of accessory muscles. The lungs revealed rales at the left
base but were otherwise clear to ascultation bilaterally with
normal breath sounds and no rubs.
.
Palpation of the heart revealed the PMI to be located in the 5th
intercostal space, mid clavicular line.  There were no thrills,
lifts or palpable S3 or S4.  The heart sounds revealed a normal
S1 and the S2 was normal. There were no rubs, murmurs, clicks or
gallops.
.
The abdominal aorta was not enlarged by palpation. There was no
hepatosplenomegaly or tenderness. The abdomen was soft nontender
and nondistended. The extremities had no pallor, cyanosis, or
clubbing; there was 1+ bilateral LE edema present. There were no
abdominal, femoral or carotid bruits. Inspection and/or
palpation of skin and subcutaneous tissue showed no stasis
dermatitis, ulcers, scars, or xanthomas.
.
Pulses:
.
Right: Carotid 2+  Femoral 2+  Popliteal 2+  DP 2+   PT 2+
Left:  Carotid 2+  Femoral 2+  Popliteal 2+  DP 2+   PT 2+


Pertinent Results:

MCV-94 MCH-31.3 MCHC-33.5 RDW-14.3 Plt Ct-286#

K-4.9 Cl-103 HCO3-27 AnGap-13

AlkPhos-102 TotBili-0.3

1981-08-07 10:45PM BLOOD TSH-1.3

2013-11-20 02:58AM BLOOD Type-ART pO2-90 pCO2-38 pH-7.44
calTCO2-27 Base XS-1
.
Discharge labs:

.
MICRO: Blood cultures 7/23,10-10 no growth
.
EKG demonstrated sinus rhythm with PVCs/PACs, normal axis, <1mm
lateral ST depressions.
.
2D-ECHOCARDIOGRAM performed on 2013-11-20 demonstrated:
The left atrium is moderately dilated. The right atrium is
moderately dilated.  There is mild symmetric left ventricular
hypertrophy with normal cavity size.  There is moderate global
left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = 35%). [Intrinsic left
ventricular systolic function is likely more depressed given the
severity of valvular regurgitation.] Right ventricular chamber
size and free wall motion are normal. The ascending aorta is
mildly dilated. The aortic valve leaflets are mildly thickened
without aortic stenosis or regurgitation. The mitral valve
leaflets are mildly thickened. Moderate (2+) mitral
regurgitation is seen. [Due to acoustic shadowing, the severity
of mitral regurgitation may be significantly UNDERestimated.]
There is borderline pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. The
pulmonic valve leaflets are thickened. Significant pulmonic
regurgitation is seen. There is a trivial/physiologic
pericardial effusion.
IMPRESSION: Biatrial enlargement. Mild symmetric left
ventricular hypertrophy with moderate global left ventricular
hypokinesis (EF 35%). Moderate mitral regurgitation. Borderline
elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressures.
.
ETT performed on 12-13 demonstrated:
10 William Kathleen protocol. Stopped d/t fatigue. Hemodynamic response
blunted in setting of beta blockade. No ischemic symptoms. At
peak exercise, 1.5-2 mm downsloping ST segment depressions in
inferolateral leads. Absent by 5 minutes of recovery.
.
CARDIAC CATH performed on 07/1983 demonstrated:
Occluded LAD w/ bridging collaterals distally from prox LAD and
acute marginal, 90% D2, LCx 40%, mid RCA occluded with bridging
collaterals distally from prox RCA, occluded PDA w/ bridging
collaterals from OM branches.
.
CXR 06-13:
1.  Left lower lobe atelectasis.  Clinical correlation is
recommended.
2.  Wedging deformity of a mid thoracic vertebral body
consistent with a superior compression fracture, age
indeterminate.  Clinical correlation is recommended.
3.  Small right pleural effusion.

Brief Hospital Course:
[**Age over 90 **] yoM w/ a h/o CAD s/p MI, CHF, PAF on coumadin who p/w 1 week
of increasing SOB and BRBPR.
.
# SOB: Likely CHF, acute pulmonary edema due to tachyarrythmia
at presentation. He was treated in the ICU with nitro gtt,
morphine, and lasix IV with resolution.  Afebrile w/o fever,
leukocytosis, or left shift to suggest infection and no gross
infiltrates observed on CXR. Unlikely PE despite asymmetric LE
swelling given INR 2.7. He ruled-out for MI. Continued CHF
treatment was given per below.
.
# GIB: The patient had BRBPR x1 at home and was guaiac positive
in the ED. He had no further bloody stools. Most likely a lower
GI source in setting of anticoagulation. Last colonoscopy 2006-08-19
with diverticulosis and a few non-bleeding colonic angioectasias
(biopsy suggestive of microscopic colitis); also EGD 2010-02-01
with hiatal hernia, Parker-David grade IV esophagitis.
Diverticuli are most likely bleeding source. GI was consulted.
Hct remained low but stable 24-26 and transfusion recommended
but the patient refused. He was intermittently guaiac negative.
Heparin and coumadin were restarted, followed by ASA. Pam Rehabilitation Hospital Of Victoria PPI
was instituted. Given stable hemodynamics and Hct, colonoscopy
was deferred to outpatient.
.
# Cardiac:
* Ischemia - s/p MI 1998. CP free except transiently in setting
of increased demand during tachycardic episodes. There was
minimal enzyme leak (TnT max 0.03) likely due to demand. ASA was
held while there was concern for bleeding but then restarted.
Continued statin, BB, ACEi.
* Pump - EF 35%, decreased from 45-50% on 12-13. BNP 4472 at
presentation. Possibly combination of ischemia and
tachycardia-induced CMP. Initially decompensated, but euvolemic
at present following IV lasix diuresis. Rate controlled per
below. Restarted coreg, ACEi. Lasix 10mg po was added.
* Rhythm - PAF with intermittent RVR to 140s and associated
chest pain. He was chemically converted on 10-10 with ibutilide
and loaded with amiodarone (normal TFTs, LFTs; will need PFTs in
1 month as outpatient). Beta-blockade was added. He was
discharged with a KOH monitor for 2 weeks that will be followed
up by Dr. Cox. Heparin gtt was continued and coumadin
restarted with discharge INR of 1.8. He was given a single dose
of lovenox prior to discharge and will have his INR rechecked on
01-18 with results faxed to Dr. Johnson for monitoring.
.
# Hyperlipidemia: Last values TC 177, LDL 85, HDL 73. Continued
lipitor.
.
# HTN: Coreg and lisinopril were restarted sequentially once
clear no significant GI bleeding. He remained normotensive.
.
# GERD: Continued PPI rathern than nexium for maximal acid
suppresion in setting of concern for GI bleed. Resumed home
regimen nexium at increased dose at discharge.
.
# s/p thyroidectomy: Benign adenoma. TSH normal. Continued
levothyroxine.
.
# Code: FULL (confirmed)
# Dispo: Home with PT
# Communication: Nathan Medina Janet Barber (wife) cell 633-625-4500

Medications on Admission:
HOME MEDICATIONS:
Carvedilol 25 mg Pam Rehabilitation Hospital Of Victoria
Diltiazem ER 120 mg daily
Aspirin 81 mg four times per week
Atorvastatin 5 mg daily
Lisinopril 40 mg daily
Nexium 40 mg daily
Warfarin 2 mg daily
Levothyroxine 175mcg PO 6X/WK (MO,TU,WE,TH,FR,SA), 88mcg 1X/WK
(Kathleen)
SL NTG prn
.
TRANFER MEDICATIONS:
Amiodarone 400 mg PO BID for 2 Weeks (Day 1=2013-11-20)
Metoprolol 5 mg IV PRN
Atorvastatin 5 mg PO DAILY
Nitroglycerin 0.05-0.2 mcg/kg/[**Month/Day/Year **] IV DRIP
Furosemide 10 mg IV ONCE
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO Q24H
Heparin IV DRIP
Ipratropium Bromide MDI 2 PUFF IH QID
Xopenex *NF* 1.36/6 mg/ml Inhalation Q4H:PRN
Levothyroxine 175mcg PO 6X/WK (MO,TU,WE,TH,FR,SA), 88mcg 1X/WK
(Kathleen)

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Yavapai Regional Medical Center - East Homecare

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary:
Congestive heart failure
Atrial fibrillation/flutter
Gastrointestinal bleeding
.
Secondary:
Coronary artery disease status post myocardial infarction
Hyperlipidemia
Hypertension
GERD
Esophageal stricture status post dilatation
History of thyroid nodule status post thyroidectomy
MGUS
History of Erlichiosis infection

Discharge Condition:
Good, afebrile, VSS

Discharge Instructions:
You were evaluated for bleeding from your GI tract, shortness of
breath, and palpitations. You bleeding stopped and you required
no blood transfusions. The shortness of breath was due to heart
failure and was treated with diuretics. You palpitations and
chest pain were caused by atrial fibrillation/flutter which was
treated with medications. You were discharged with a heart
monitor that you will wear as directed for 2 weeks.
.
Please take all medications as prescribed.
New medications: amiodarone, lasix
Changed medications: coreg, lisinopril, coumadin
Discontinued medications: diltiazem
.
Call your doctor or return to the ED immediately if you
experience worsening chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea,
vomiting, abdominal pain, sweating, fevers, chills, bleeding, or
other concerning symptoms.

Followup Instructions:
You will need to have your coumadin level (INR) checked on
Tuesday 2002-01-03 at your outpatient lab. The results should be
sent to Dr. Johnson.
.
